summary: Joel argues that every software team must use a bug tracking database, since no one can reliably remember bugs. He walks through the lifecycle of a sample bug from discovery through resolution, demonstrating proper workflow between testers and developers. The post establishes three essential components of every good bug report: steps to reproduce, expected behavior, and actual behavior. Joel emphasizes that only the person who opened a bug should be allowed to close it, and that bugs must always be assigned to exactly one person. He closes with ten practical tips for getting teams to adopt bug tracking, warning especially against feature creep in the bug database itself.
key_points: Every good bug report requires exactly three things: steps to reproduce, what you expected to see, and what you saw instead
A bug must always be assigned to exactly one person, and only the person who opened the bug can close it — developers can resolve, but reporters confirm
Good testers reduce repro steps to the minimal set needed to trigger the bug, which is extremely helpful for programmers
Resist the temptation to add new fields to the bug database — complexity in the entry form drives people away from using it
If people won't use the bug database, refuse to accept bug reports through any other channel
money_quotes: I was born with only two bug-storing-slots in my brain. At any given time, I can only remember two bugs.
One of the biggest incorrect facts that programmers consistently seem to believe is that they can remember all their bugs or keep them on post-it notes.
Every good bug report needs exactly three things: steps to reproduce, what you expected to see, and what you saw instead.
A bug is like a hot potato: when it's assigned to you, you are responsible to resolve it, somehow, or assign it to someone else.
The golden rule is that only the person who opened the bug can close the bug.
If you're a programmer, and you're having trouble getting testers to use the bug database, just don't accept bug reports by any other method.
If entering bugs 'formally' is too much work, people will go around the bug database.
If you are developing code, even on a team of one, without an organized database listing all known bugs in the code, you are simply going to ship low quality code.
key_insight: Bug tracking succeeds only when the process is lightweight enough that everyone actually uses it, with clear ownership rules ensuring bugs cannot be swept under the rug.
